concerns.text: Plan acknowledges 'Specialization index and retrieval efficiency may be definitionally related (both derived from fact-contribution distributions)' but proceeds to treat them as independent dependent variables in ANOVA. This creates circular validation where the two outcome metrics are not empirically independent.

Cue-retrieval efficiency uses 1/N_agents as uniform-chance baseline, but transactive-memory theory predicts specialization (non-uniform distribution). The metric may be tautologically designed to show improvement over a baseline the theory predicts shouldn't exist.

Two-way ANOVA treats 'Metric' (specialization, retrieval) as a between-subjects factor, but both metrics are measured on the same games. This violates independence assumptions; should be mixed-design ANOVA or separate analyses.

All primary datasets (Hanabi, CoQA) lack verified sources; implementation falls back to synthetic cue/fact generation. Synthetic data may not capture real transactive-memory dynamics, limiting external validity of claims about 'collective remembering'.
